skill.name: uml-modeling
skill.description: Design, critique, and revise UML diagrams from a modeling and communication perspective. Use when Codex is asked to create or improve UML; when the user asks for a graphical representation, diagram, schema, visual model, process map, state view, architecture view, or system representation of software/system behavior or structure; when the user does not explicitly choose UML but needs a model-like visual explanation; when Codex must autonomously choose the right UML diagram type or split across multiple UML diagrams. Use for reasoning about diagram form, abstraction level, boundaries, grouping, lifecycle/state design, behavior vs structure, interaction design, or diagnosing why a diagram feels wrong at the modeling level. This skill treats notation as the final representation, not as the core task.
skill.summary: Design semantically correct UML diagrams by choosing the right diagram type, modeling concepts before notation, and validating against UML semantics.
skill.author: Carbocode

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported 401 potential issues, but every finding is a false positive. The skill contains only markdown documentation with UML modeling guidance. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are UML state labels and arrow syntax inside backtick code spans. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detections are markdown inline code blocks. The 'system reconnaissance' detections are legitimate UML modeling terms such as 'network', 'deployment', and 'service reachable'. No executable code, network calls, file system access, or malicious intent was found.

content.user_title: Create precise UML diagrams for any software design challenge
content.value_statement: Teams struggle to choose the right diagram type and end up with confusing models that mix structure, behavior, and interaction. This skill guides you to select the correct UML form, build semantically accurate models, and produce diagrams that communicate clearly to any audience.
actual_capabilities: Select the correct UML diagram type based on the modeling question
Design class, sequence, state machine, activity, use case, and deployment diagrams
Critique existing diagrams for semantic errors and modeling anti-patterns
Convert informal diagram requests into proper UML models
Apply UML 2.5.1 semantics to ensure notation matches meaning
Split complex models across multiple diagrams when one would distort the message
limitations: Does not generate images or visual renderings; produces text-based notation
Requires the user to provide domain knowledge about the system being modeled
Cannot execute code or interact with external modeling tools
Assumes the user wants standard UML; custom notations need explicit instruction

use_cases.title: Design a class diagram for a new API
use_cases.description: A backend developer needs to model entities, relationships, and multiplicities before writing code. The skill helps choose the right abstraction level and avoid common class diagram anti-patterns.

use_cases.title: Model a user lifecycle with state machines
use_cases.description: A product manager wants to visualize how a user account moves through states such as pending, active, suspended, and closed. The skill ensures states represent conditions, not actions.

use_cases.title: Document service interactions with sequence diagrams
use_cases.description: A system architect needs to show message order between microservices during a checkout flow. The skill designs the diagram so temporal relationships are clear and complete.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the right diagram type
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to model how a user registration flow works. Should I use an activity diagram, a sequence diagram, or a state machine? Explain which UML diagram type fits best and why.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner unsure which UML form to use

prompt_templates.title: Create a class diagram
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a class diagram for an e-commerce system with customers, orders, products, and payments. Show the key relationships, multiplicities, and any composition or aggregation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user designing a domain model

prompt_templates.title: Critique an existing diagram
prompt_templates.prompt: Here is a state machine diagram I created. Can you review it for semantic correctness? [paste diagram]. Point out any states that should be actions, mixed subjects, or missing transitions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user validating their own work

prompt_templates.title: Design a multi-diagram architecture model
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to document a distributed system with three microservices, a message queue, and a database. Create a component diagram for the topology, a sequence diagram for the main flow, and a deployment diagram for the infrastructure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user documenting a full architecture

output_examples.input: Create a state machine for an order lifecycle
output_examples.output:
Chosen diagram type: State Machine Diagram. Subject: order.

States:
- Draft
- Submitted
- Approved
- Fulfilled
- Cancelled

Transitions:
- Draft --> Submitted : submit
- Submitted --> Approved : approve [payment valid] / reserve inventory
- Submitted --> Cancelled : reject
- Approved --> Fulfilled : ship / send notification
- Approved --> Cancelled : cancel [before ship] / release inventory

Note: All states are conditions that can persist. 'Reserve inventory' is a transition effect, not a state.

output_examples.input: Model a login flow with activity diagram
output_examples.output:
Chosen diagram type: Activity Diagram.

Actions (in order):
1. Start: Enter credentials
2. Validate input format
3. Decision: Credentials valid?
   - no --> Show error --> Stop
   - yes --> Authenticate user
4. Decision: Authentication successful?
   - no --> Show error --> Stop
   - yes --> Create session
5. Redirect to dashboard
6. Stop

Partitions: User | Application | Authentication Service

best_practices: Always define the diagram's audience and the single question it must answer before drawing
Model semantics before choosing notation or layout; a correct model in text is better than a wrong model in a fancy format
Keep each diagram at one abstraction level; do not mix conceptual and implementation details
anti_patterns: Mixing structure, behavior, and interaction in a single diagram instead of splitting across multiple UML types
Using states as actions or actions as states; remember that states are conditions that can persist
Including meta-information such as 'as requested', 'current project', or 'TODO' inside diagram labels

faq.question: Does this skill generate visual images of diagrams?
faq.answer: No. This skill produces text-based UML notation and semantic guidance. You can paste the output into tools such as PlantUML, Mermaid, or drawing software to create visual diagrams.

faq.question: Which UML diagram should I use for a user login flow?
faq.answer: Use an activity diagram if you want to show the sequence of steps and decisions. Use a sequence diagram if you want to show how the user, frontend, and backend services interact over time. Use a state machine if you want to model the lifecycle of a user session.

faq.question: Can this skill help me improve an existing diagram?
faq.answer: Yes. Share your existing diagram and the skill will critique it for semantic errors, wrong diagram type choice, mixed abstraction levels, and notation issues. It will then suggest repairs or a redesign.

faq.question: What is the difference between a class diagram and an object diagram?
faq.answer: A class diagram shows types, their features, and structural relationships. An object diagram shows a concrete snapshot of instances and links at a specific moment in time. Use a class diagram for design; use an object diagram to illustrate or debug a class model.

faq.question: Does the skill support custom or non-standard UML notation?
faq.answer: The skill follows standard UML 2.5.1 semantics. If you need a custom notation, state your requirements explicitly. The skill will warn you when a requested notation distorts the underlying UML model.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for non-software diagrams like business processes?
faq.answer: Yes. Activity diagrams and use case diagrams work well for business processes and user goals. The skill will adapt the modeling guidance to your domain while keeping the UML semantics correct.
